text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the pars flaccida of the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the right crura. The peritoneal covering is retracted, and the vessel sealer is used to score down to the muscle fibers, facilitating the dissection of the hernia sac from mediastinal structures. The anterior covering on the crura is left intact. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus using an avascular plane, ensuring preservation of the anterior vagus nerve. The left crura is scored similarly, opening the peritoneum overlying the crura. The surgeon spreads in the avascular plane on the anterior and right side of the esophagus, dividing vessels as needed while preserving the vagus nerve. A posterior window is created by gently spreading the peritoneal covering at the confluence of the crura, allowing placement of a Penrose drain around the esophagus for retraction. The posterior dissection is performed with careful visualization of the tissue plane over the aorta, identifying the posterior vagus nerve before division. The esophagus is lifted and vessels are divided, achieving a high dissection and mobilization of the esophagus with intra-abdominal length. The crura is closed with a V-lock suture, ensuring appropriate tension and closure without impinging on the esophagus.
The surgeon places a Phasix ST mesh over the crural closure site in a parasophageal hernia repair, securing it with Vicryl sutures to provide additional support for the closure.

The surgeon retracts tissue adjacent to the crura and scores the peritoneal covering down to the crural muscle, opening up the cruse along its length while preserving the anterior covering. The hernia sac is held in the right hand, and dissection occurs outside of it using avascular planes. The camera is adjusted to a thirty-degree angle for better visualization, aiding in the reduction of the hernia sac. The posterior window is created around the distal esophagus, avoiding excess stomach in the Penrose retractor. The camera is driven high into the mediastinum to visualize the posterior plane between the esophagus and the aorta. Circumferential dissection around the esophagus is performed to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length. The cruse is closed with a V-lock suture, addressing wide spacing and tension by suturing from the right lateral position to re-approximate the cruse to the esophagus.
